content.text: A policy alias is a string path that Azure Policy uses to reference a specific property of an ARM resource. When you write a field condition in a policy rule — such as "field": "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/storageProfile.osDisk.osType" — the string after "field" is an alias. Aliases are maintained by Azure and versioned per API version. Without knowing the correct alias string, you cannot author a policy that targets a specific resource property.

attrs.alt: Azure Policy alias anatomy and namespace mapping

content.text: Every alias has a namespace prefix matching the ARM resource provider (Microsoft.Compute, Microsoft.Storage, etc.), followed by the resource type, and then the property path within that resource type. A small set of built-in aliases (type, name, location, tags) require no namespace prefix and work across all resource types.

Aliases are not the same as ARM property paths in REST API responses. Some properties exist in the REST response but have no policy alias — they cannot be evaluated in a policy condition. Always verify an alias exists with az policy alias list before using it.

The az policy alias list command is the primary discovery tool. It returns all aliases registered for a given resource provider and type, along with metadata about whether the alias is modifiable (required for Append and Modify effects) and whether it is an array expression (requiring a count expression).

# List all aliases for a resource type
az policy alias list \
  --namespace Microsoft.Compute \
  --resource-type virtualMachines \
  --query '[].{Alias:name,Modifiable:defaultMetadata.attributes}' \
  -o table | head -30

# Search aliases by keyword
az policy alias list \
  --namespace Microsoft.Storage \
  --resource-type storageAccounts \
  --query "[?contains(name, 'https')].name" -o tsv

# Find modifiable aliases only (required for Modify effect)
az policy alias list \
  --namespace Microsoft.Compute \
  --resource-type virtualMachines \
  --query "[?defaultMetadata.attributes=='Modifiable'].name" -o tsv | head -20

# Find array expression aliases (require count expression in policy rules)
az policy alias list \
  --namespace Microsoft.Network \
  --resource-type networkSecurityGroups \
  --query "[?defaultMetadata.attributes=='arrayExpression'].name" -o tsv

# Check a specific alias exists and get its API version support
az policy alias list \
  --namespace Microsoft.KeyVault \
  --resource-type vaults \
  --query "[?name=='Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/enableSoftDelete'].{Name:name,APIs:pathMetadata[].apiVersions[0]}" \
  -o json

The most important alias attribute is whether the property is a scalar value (string, integer, boolean) or an array. Using the wrong approach for an array alias is a common authoring mistake that results in a policy that either never matches or always matches incorrectly.
field + comparison operator (equals, in, exists, like)
Do not use count expression — unnecessary and causes errors
count expression with [*] and where clause; compare count to integer
Do not use equals/in directly against the array alias — will not match correctly
Individual element inside an array (used inside count where)
field with [*] inside the where clause of a count expression
Do not use as top-level field outside a count expression
The value Condition: When No Alias Exists

Some properties the policy needs to test have no registered alias — the ARM REST API exposes the data but Azure Policy has not published an alias for it. In these cases, the value condition provides an escape hatch: instead of a field path, you provide an ARM template function expression that evaluates to a testable string. The value is the computed string, and the operator tests it.
Array of security rules (use count expression)
Alias Versioning and API Version Caveats

Azure Policy aliases are versioned per API version. A property that exists in API version 2022-11-01 may not be available (or may have a different alias path) in an older API version. When a resource is deployed with an older API version, the policy engine evaluates it against the aliases available for that API version. The mismatch can cause a policy to produce 'Not Started' or appear to not evaluate correctly.

# Check which API versions support a specific alias
az policy alias list \
  --namespace Microsoft.KeyVault \
  --resource-type vaults \
  --query "[?name=='Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/enableSoftDelete'].pathMetadata" \
  -o json
# Output shows the alias path and the list of API versions where it is available

# When writing a policy, prefer aliases available in the widest range of API versions
# Check what API version your Terraform provider or ARM template uses:
az provider show --namespace Microsoft.KeyVault --query 'resourceTypes[?resourceType==`vaults`].apiVersions[0]' -o tsv

# If a new alias is only available in recent API versions, add an API version
# filter to the policy condition to avoid false NonCompliant on old deployments:
{
  "allOf": [
    { "field": "type", "equals": "Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults" },
    {
      "field": "Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/enablePurgeProtection",
      "exists": "true"
      // Only evaluates if the property exists in the deployed API version
    }
  ]
}
Testing Aliases Before Deploying a Policy

Testing a policy alias before creating the policy definition saves significant debugging time. The ARM What-If operation and the Azure Policy compliance scan both evaluate the full policyRule. A quicker first test is to use the az policy alias list output to confirm the alias exists, then craft a minimal Audit policy targeting a single known resource to verify the match behavior before assigning at scale.

# Step 1: Confirm the alias you want exists
az policy alias list \
  --namespace Microsoft.Storage \
  --resource-type storageAccounts \
  --query "[?name=='Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/networkAcls.defaultAction']" \
  -o json

# Step 2: Create a test policy with Audit effect (safe — never blocks anything)
az policy definition create \
  --name test-storage-public-access \
  --mode Indexed \
  --rules '{
    "if": {
      "allOf": [
        { "field": "type", "equals": "Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts" },
        { "field": "Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/networkAcls.defaultAction", "notEquals": "Deny" }
      ]
    },
    "then": { "effect": "audit" }
  }' \
  --params '{}'

# Step 3: Assign to a test subscription or resource group
az policy assignment create \
  --name test-storage-public-access \
  --policy test-storage-public-access \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-test

# Step 4: Trigger evaluation on the test scope
az policy state trigger-scan --subscription $SUB_ID

# Step 5: Check which storage accounts are flagged
az policy state list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq 'test-storage-public-access' and complianceState eq 'NonCompliant'" \
  --select resourceId \
  -o tsv

# Step 6: Verify the flagged resources actually have the wrong setting
az storage account list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --query "[?networkRuleSet.defaultAction!='Deny'].{Name:name,Action:networkRuleSet.defaultAction}" \
  -o table
# If both lists match, the alias is working correctly

# Step 7: Clean up the test assignment and definition
az policy assignment delete --name test-storage-public-access --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-test
az policy definition delete --name test-storage-public-access
Using Aliases with the Modify Effect

The Modify effect sets or updates resource properties during ARM operations. It requires the alias to be modifiable — if you try to use a readonly alias in a Modify operation field, the policy definition will be rejected at creation time. The operations array inside details supports three operation types: addOrReplace (set the value regardless of existing value), add (set only if not present), and remove (delete the property or array element).
Aliases for Diagnostic Settings and Monitoring Resources

Diagnostic settings (Microsoft.Insights/diagnosticSettings) are companion resources that live on top of another resource. When writing an AuditIfNotExists or DeployIfNotExists policy to enforce diagnostic settings, the existence check needs to reference aliases within the diagnosticSettings resource type. These aliases cover the logs array, metrics array, workspace ID, storage account ID, and event hub. Getting these aliases correct is critical for accurate existenceCondition logic.

# Discover all diagnostic settings aliases
az policy alias list \
  --namespace Microsoft.Insights \
  --resource-type diagnosticSettings \
  --query '[].{Alias:name,Attr:defaultMetadata.attributes}' \
  -o table

# Key aliases for diagnostic settings existence checks:
#
# Microsoft.Insights/diagnosticSettings/workspaceId
#   -> the Log Analytics workspace resource ID (string, noneArray, modifiable)
#
# Microsoft.Insights/diagnosticSettings/storageAccountId
#   -> the storage account resource ID for archive
#
# Microsoft.Insights/diagnosticSettings/logs[*]
#   -> array of log category configurations (arrayExpression)
#
# Microsoft.Insights/diagnosticSettings/logs[*].category
#   -> specific log category name (arrayMember — use inside count where)
#
# Microsoft.Insights/diagnosticSettings/logs[*].enabled
#   -> whether the log category is enabled (arrayMember)
#
# Microsoft.Insights/diagnosticSettings/metrics[*].enabled
#   -> whether metrics collection is enabled (arrayMember)

# Validate your existenceCondition aliases work correctly:
# Test: does a KV have a diagnostic setting pointing to the right workspace?
az policy state list \
  --resource /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/$RG/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/$VAULT_NAME \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq 'require-kv-diag-settings'" \
  --query '[].{State:complianceState,Reason:reasons[0].code}' \
  -o table
ARM Template Functions Available in policyRule

A subset of ARM template functions can be used in both field and value conditions, and in the then.details.deployment.template section. These functions are evaluated at policy evaluation time (for conditions) or at deployment time (for DINE templates). Using the wrong function in the wrong context causes silent evaluation failures.
Stamp creation date in Modify or DINE templates
Build resource ID inside a DINE ARM template
Pass the evaluated resource's property into DINE deployment parameters

Policy aliases are string property paths that map to ARM resource properties and are used in the field key of a policy condition. Built-in aliases (type, name, location, tags) work across all resource types; provider-specific aliases follow the namespace/resourceType/propertyPath pattern. Use az policy alias list to discover correct aliases and their attributes (scalar noneArray vs array arrayExpression). Array aliases require a count expression with [*] iteration — using them with direct operators produces incorrect results. The value condition is the fallback when no field alias exists, enabling ARM function expressions like resourceGroup().tags['key']. Always test a new alias with an Audit-effect policy on a test scope before deploying at scale.
